Codeforces Round 1084 (Div. 3) Editorial
By AksLolCoding, 4 weeks ago,

Thanks to everyone who participated in this round! I hope you enjoyed the contest.

Rate the contest!
  • 12 Horrible contest
  • 3 Bad contest
  • 17 Average contest
  • 198 Good contest

Rate the difficulty!
  • 4 Extreme contest
  • 46 Hard contest

2200A - Eating Game

Rate the problem!
  • 6 Horrible problem
  • 13 Bad problem
  • 144 Average problem
  • 29 Good problem
  • 6 Peak problem
  • 7 Did not solve
Tutorial

2200A - Eating Game

Let mm be the maximum value in aa. After n(m−1)n(m−1) moves, only players with ai=mai=m will have dishes remaining, so no other players can win. If ai=mai=m, then player ii can win if the first player is player (imodn)+1(imodn)+1. Hence, the answer is the number of maximums of aa.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for (auto &i: a) cin >> i;
    
    int mx = 0, ans = 0;
    for (int i : a) mx = max(i, mx);
    for (int i : a) ans += i == mx;
    cout << ans << '\n';
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200B - Deletion Sort

Rate the problem!
  • 11 Horrible problem
  • 2 Bad problem
  • 89 Average problem
  • 81 Good problem
  • 4 Peak problem
  • 6 Did not solve
Tutorial

2200B - Deletion Sort

If aa is non-decreasing at the start, then the answer is trivially nn. Otherwise, there exists an ii such that ai>ai+1ai>ai+1. We can delete all elements besides these two and then delete aiai to get to 11 remaining element, so the answer is 11 in this case.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for (auto &i: a) cin >> i;

    if (is_sorted(a.begin(), a.end())) cout << n << '\n';
    else cout << "1\n";
}

signed main() {
    cin.tie(0)->sync_with_stdio(0);
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200C - Specialty String

Rate the problem!
  • 26 Horrible problem
  • 4 Bad problem
  • 69 Average problem
  • 103 Good problem
  • 19 Peak problem
  • 6 Did not solve
Hint

What if you delete characters instead of replacing them with ∗∗?

Tutorial

2200C - Specialty String

Instead of replacing characters with *s, just delete them from the string. Then, the operation becomes deleting two adjacent characters that are equal. AksLolCoding wins if the string becomes empty. Since deleting a pair can never disrupt another pair, the order in which we do the operations does not matter.

We search through the array n2n2 times, removing a pair of equal elements whenever we find one. At the end, just output whether the string is empty.

The problem can also be solved in O(n)O(n) using a stack.

Code 1 (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    string s;
    cin >> n >> s;
    for (int i = 0; 2*i < n; i++) {
        for (int j = 1; j < size(s); j++) {
            if (s[j] == s[j-1]) {
                s.erase(j-1, 2);
            }
        }
    }
    cout << (s.empty() ? "YES\n" : "NO\n");
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}
Code 2 (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n;
    string s;
    cin >> n >> s;
    stack<int> st;
    for (char c : s) {
        if (st.size() && c == st.top()) st.pop();
        else st.push(c);
    }
    cout << (st.empty() ? "YES\n" : "NO\n");
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200D - Portal

Rate the problem!
  • 78 Horrible problem
  • 12 Bad problem
  • 16 Average problem
  • 188 Good problem
  • 39 Peak problem
  • 34 Did not solve
Hint 1

What invariants does pp have?

Hint 2

Consider the elements in between the portals and the elements outside of them.

Hint 3

What invariant does each of these two regions have?

Tutorial

2200D - Portal

Let AA denote the subarray of elements between the two portals and BB denote the remaining elements. The operations can cyclically shift AA. They cannot change the order of elements of BB, but they can change the position of AA within BB. Importantly, an element cannot move from AA to BB or vice versa.

To make the lexicographically minimum permutation, we should cyclically shift AA until its leftmost element is its minimum element. Then, insert AA to the left of the leftmost element in BB that is greater than the minimum of AA (or after AA if no such element exists).

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

void solve() {
    int n, x, y;
    cin >> n >> x >> y;
    x--, y--;
    vector<int> a, b;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        int j;
        cin >> j;
        if (i <= x || i > y) a.push_back(j);
        else b.push_back(j);
    }

    // solve
    if (!b.empty()) rotate(b.begin(), min_element(b.begin(), b.end()), b.end());
    int m = b.empty() ? -1 : b[0];
    auto it = a.begin();
    while (it != a.end() && *it < m) it++;
    a.insert(it, b.begin(), b.end());
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cout << a[i] << " \n"[i == n-1];
    }
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200E - Divisive Battle

Rate the problem!
  • 15 Horrible problem
  • 2 Bad problem
  • 10 Average problem
  • 170 Good problem
  • 44 Peak problem
  • 16 Did not solve
Hint 1

What elements can the operation not be used on?

Answer

11 and prime numbers.

Hint 2

What first moves ensure that Alice will win?

Answer

Making a small prime number appear in the array after a number with a larger prime factor.

Hint 3

If Alice can win, then she can ensure victory on her first move.

Tutorial

2200E - Divisive Battle

If the aa is non-decreasing, Bob wins immediately. Otherwise:

If there exists an element xx in aa that is divisible by two distinct primes p<qp<q, then Alice can ensure her victory by replacing xx with xpxp and pp. Since xpxp is a multiple of qq, there will always be a multiple of qq that appears before pp and is greater than pp, so the array will never be non-decreasing.

If no such element exists, all elements in the array are either 11 or prime powers of the form pepe for some prime pp. Define an array bb such that bibi is 11 if ai=1ai=1 and is the unique prime factor of aiai otherwise. If bibi is non-decreasing, then Bob will win (since aa will be non-decreasing once no moves are possible). Otherwise, there exists an ii such that bi−1>bibi−1>bi. Alice can replace ai=peai=pe with pp and pe−1pe−1 if e>1e>1. By similar logic to the above paragraph, this will make it impossible for Bob to win, so Alice will win.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

#define all(x) begin(x), end(x)

int primebase(int x) {
    set<int> s;
    for (int i = 2; i*i <= x; i++) {
        while (x % i == 0) {
            s.insert(i);
            x /= i;
        }
    }
    if (x > 1) s.insert(x);
    if (s.size() > 1) return -1;
    if (s.size() == 0) return 1;
    return *s.begin();
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<int> a(n);
    for (auto &i: a) cin >> i;
    
    // solve
    vector<int> b(n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) b[i] = primebase(a[i]);
    if (is_sorted(all(a))) {
        cout << "Bob\n";
    } else if (*min_element(all(b)) == -1) {
        cout << "Alice\n";
    } else if (is_sorted(all(b))) {
        cout << "Bob\n";
    } else {
        cout << "Alice\n";
    }
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200F - Mooclear Reactor 2

Rate the problem!
  • 18 Horrible problem
  • 0 Bad problem
  • 5 Average problem
  • 28 Good problem
  • 120 Peak problem
  • 30 Did not solve
Hint 1

Try to solve the problem without shop particles.

Hint 1.1

Fix the number of energy-generating particles kk. Which particles can Bessie use?

Answer

She can use all particles with y≥k−1y≥k−1.

Hint 1.2

Of the particles she can use for some kk, which should Bessie use?

Answer

The kk particles with the largest xx values.

Hint 1.3

Process kk in decreasing order.

Hint 2

If Bessie has to use a shop particle, which of her own particles should she replace with it?

Answer

The energy-generating particle with smallest xx.

Tutorial

2200F - Mooclear Reactor 2

Consider solving the problem without any shop particles. We can iterate on the number of energy generating particles kk from nn to 11. We can only use particles with y≥k−1y≥k−1. Store a set or min heap of the kk largest xx values. As kk decreases, we add particles to the set/heap, removing the smallest element whenever the size of the set/heap exceeds kk. We can store the sum of the elements in the set/heap in a variable ss. The answer aa will be the max of ss over all values of kk.

To handle shop particles, we can run the algorithm again, but using a maximum set size of k−1k−1 instead of kk, reserving an extra slot for the shop particle. We can store the sum ss for each kk in an array ff. We can then take the prefix max of ff by setting f[k]:=max(f[k],f[k−1])f[k]:=max(f[k],f[k−1]). For each shop particle, we can:

  • choose to not use it, resulting in aa energy, or
  • pair it with any other subset of size at most y+1y+1, resulting in x+f[y+1]x+f[y+1] energy.

The answer for each shop particle is just the maximum of these two values.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
using ll = long long;

template<class T> bool smin(T &a, const T b) { return b < a ? a = b, 1 : 0;}
template<class T> bool smax(T &a, const T b) { return a < b ? a = b, 1 : 0;}

void solve() {
    int n, q;
    cin >> n >> q;
    vector<array<int, 2>> a(n);
    for (auto &i : a) cin >> i[1] >> i[0];

    // solve
    sort(a.rbegin(), a.rend());
    int ptr = 0;
    multiset<int> s;
    ll mx = 0, cur = 0;
    vector<ll> pref(n+1);
    for (int k = n; k >= 0; k--) {
        while (ptr < n && a[ptr][0] >= k) {
            cur += a[ptr][1];
            s.insert(a[ptr][1]);
            ptr++;
        }
        while (s.size() > k + 1) {
            cur -= *s.begin();
            s.erase(s.begin());
        }
        smax(mx, cur);
        pref[k] = (s.size() <= k) ? cur : cur - *s.begin();
    }
    for (int k = 1; k <= n; k++) smax(pref[k], pref[k-1]);

    // queries
    while (q--) {
        int x, y;
        cin >> x >> y;
        cout << max(mx, pref[y] + x) << '\n';
    }
}

signed main() {
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

2200G - Operation Permutation

Rate the problem!
  • 1 Horrible problem
  • 2 Bad problem
  • 1 Average problem
  • 17 Good problem
  • 36 Peak problem
  • 30 Did not solve
Hint 1

Convert each - operation to + and / operation to x.

Hint 2

Fix the permutation. For each +y operation, by how much would the answer change if it was removed?

Answer

The answer would reduce by the product of yy and the x operations that come after it in the permutation.

Hint 3

How can you calculate the expected value of the product x operations that come after +y over all permutations?

Tutorial

2200G - Operation Permutation

Replace each - operation with +(-y) and /y with x(1/y) (mod109+7)(mod109+7).

Consider the contribution of each +y operation to the final answer. Its contribution is equal to the product of yy and the expected value of the product of x operations that come after it. Let there be mm x operations in total. Then, since all permutations are equally likely, there is a 1m+11m+1 chance for there to be kk x operations after the +y operation for all 0≤k≤m.0≤k≤m.

For each kk, we need to compute the expected value of the sum of a random subset of kk of the mm x operations. To do this, we can use a DP where dp[i][j]dp[i][j] is the sum of the products of all subsets of size jj using only the first ii x operations. If bibi is the value of the ii-th x operation, then the DP transition is dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j]+bi⋅dp[i−1][j−1]dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j]+bi⋅dp[i−1][j−1]. Calculating dpdp takes O(m2)O(m2) time.

The expected value of the sum of a random subset of kk x operations is dp[m][k](mk)dp[m][k](mk), so the expected value of the product of the x operations after a + operation is

S=1m+1∑k=0mdp[m][k](mk).S=1m+1∑k=0mdp[m][k](mk).

For each +y operation, its contribution to the final answer is S⋅yS⋅y. After summing contributions of the +y terms, we just need to add the contribution of xx, which is x⋅dp[m][m]x⋅dp[m][m], to get the final answer.

The time complexity is O(n2)O(n2) since m≤nm≤n.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
using ll = long long;

const int mod = 1e9+7;

ll mpow(ll a, ll b) {
    ll r = 1;
    while (b) {
        if (b & 1) {
            r *= a;
            r %= mod;
        }
        b >>= 1;
        a *= a;
        a %= mod;
    }
    return r;
}

ll inv(ll a) {
    return mpow(a, mod-2);
}

const int maxn = 5000;
ll facts[maxn+1] = {1};

void precomp() {
    for (int i = 1; i <= maxn; i++) {
        facts[i] = facts[i-1] * i % mod;
    }
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    ll x;
    cin >> n >> x;

    vector<ll> mults;
    ll adds = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        char c;
        ll v;
        cin >> c >> v;
        if (c == '-') adds += mod - v;
        else if (c == '+') adds += v;
        else if (c == '/') mults.push_back(inv(v));
        else mults.push_back(v);
    }
    adds %= mod;

    // solve
    int m = mults.size();
    vector<ll> dp(m+1);
    dp[0] = 1;
    for (auto &b : mults) {
        x *= b;
        x %= mod;
        for (int i = m; i >= 1; i--) {
            dp[i] += dp[i-1] * b;
            dp[i] %= mod;
        }
    }

    ll S = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i <= m; i++) {
        S += dp[i] * facts[i] % mod * facts[m-i] % mod * inv(facts[m]) % mod;
        S %= mod;
    }
    S *= inv(m+1);
    S %= mod;

    // answer
    ll ans = x + adds * S;
    ans %= mod;
    cout << ans << "\n";
}

signed main() {
    precomp();
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}
Bonus from Proof_by_QED

Solve for n≤2⋅105n≤2⋅105.

2200H - Six Seven

Rate the problem!
  • 8 Horrible problem
  • 0 Bad problem
  • 2 Average problem
  • 5 Good problem
  • 42 Peak problem
  • 27 Did not solve
Hint 1

Find a recursive definition for special numbers.

Hint 2

When can xmod42xmod42 tell us if xx is special?

Answer

Whenever 42∤x42∤x.

Hint 3

If 42∣x42∣x, how can we determine if xx is special?

Answer

xx will be special if and only if x42x42 is special.

Hint 4

Iterate on the number of operations mod42mod42.

Tutorial

2200H - Six Seven

Consider a recursive definition of being special. If a number xx is not divisible by 4242, it is special if and only if it is divisible by 66. Otherwise, since f6(x42)=f6(x)−1f6(x42)=f6(x)−1 and f7(x42)=f7(x)−1f7(x42)=f7(x)−1, xx is special if and only if x42x42 is special.

Let the number of operations performed be mm. For each value of k=mmod42k=mmod42, let us find the minimum possible mm. For each ii, if 42∤ai+k42∤ai+k, this value of kk is impossible unless 66 divides ai+kai+k. Otherwise, ai+m42ai+m42 must be special. Create an array bb of the values of ai+k42ai+k42 for all ii such that 42∣ai+k42∣ai+k. Finding the minimum mm congruent to kmod42kmod42 is equivalent to solving the original problem for the array bb, so we can use recursion to find m′m′, the minimum number of operations to make all elements in bb special. (Make sure to create a base case in the recursion!) Then, the minimum mm for this case is m=42m′+km=42m′+k. Our final answer is just the minimum of these values over all possible kk.

To see why this runs in the time limit, observe that each aiai corresponds to exactly one array element at each recursion depth. Hence, each recursion depth is processed in a total of O(n)O(n) time. Since each recursion divides values by 4242 compared to the previous recursion, the maximum recursion depth is O(log42109)O(log42⁡109). Thus, the total time complexity is O(nlog42109)O(nlog42⁡109), though it may vary based on implementation.

Code (C++)
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

using ll = long long;
const ll inf = LLONG_MAX / 2;

ll f(const vector<ll>& a) {
    if (a.empty()) return 0;
    if (a.back() == 1) return 5;
    int n = a.size();
    int mod6 = a[0] % 6;
    vector<ll> groups[7];
    for (ll x : a) {
        if (x % 6 != mod6) return inf;
        groups[(x - mod6) / 6 % 7].push_back(x);
    }
    ll ans = inf;
    for (int r = 0; r < 7; r++) {
        ll k = (42 - 6*r - mod6) % 42;
        vector<ll> next;
        for (ll x : groups[r]) next.push_back((x + k) / 42);
        ll rec = f(next);
        if (rec >= inf) continue;
        ans = min(ans, k + 42 * rec);
    }
    return ans;
}

void solve() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<ll> a(n);
    for (auto &x : a) cin >> x;
    sort(a.begin(), a.end());
    ll res = f(a);
    if (res >= inf) cout << "-1\n";
    else cout << res << '\n';
}
signed main() {
    cin.tie(0)->sync_with_stdio(0);
    int t = 1;
    cin >> t;
    while (t--) solve();
}

+20

Solution to G bonus

Spoiler

Instead of doing DP, model the multiplication as terms in polynomial coefficients (1+b1⋅x)(1+b2⋅x)(1+b3⋅x)...(1+bm⋅x)(1+b1⋅x)(1+b2⋅x)(1+b3⋅x)...(1+bm⋅x)

We can see that the coefficient of xkxk in the dp(m,k)dp(m,k) in the DP described in the editorial

Calculating this from left to right still takes O(m2)O(m2) time. But you can instead just pair up the linear terms to get quadratic terms, then pair up the terms to get quartic terms, and so on. This takes O(mlog2(m))O(mlog2⁡(m)) time instead.

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    Anush2010
    4 weeks ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    Anush2010
    4 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    Can you explain what is S in terms of probability and why we multiply everything by 1/(m+1)

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      cauemb
      3 weeks ago, show (+1) #
      »
      »
      »
      cauemb
      3 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      We are trying to calculate the expected value (EV) of the final value of the expression. To do this, by linearity of expectation, we can calculate the expected value of each additive term separately and sum them all to obtain the final answer.

      At the end of an arbitrary expression, an additive term yy will have the form y⋅Xy⋅X, where XX is the product of all multiplicative terms to the right of yy. There may be many possible values for XX, but only its expected value matters to us.

      Let SS denote E[X]E[X], the expected value of XX. The contribution to the answer for each additive term yy is, therefore, S.yS.y. We now compute SS.

      Again, by linearity of expectation (with a small twist), we have: S=∑mk=0Pr(there are k multiplicative terms to the right of y)⋅E[product if there are k multiplicative terms to the right of y].S=∑k=0mPr(there are k multiplicative terms to the right of y)⋅E[product if there are k multiplicative terms to the right of y].

      Each probability term is 1m+11m+1 due to randomness, and each expected product term corresponds to the dp/choosedp/choose component.

      That's how the editorial finds S=1m+1∑mk=0dp[m][k]/(mk)S=1m+1∑k=0mdp[m][k]/(mk)

      If you, as I did until yesterday, struggle with comprehending Expected Values and it's manipulations that were used, i recommend you watch Errichto 's lecture on YouTube

      → Reply

    Yes I think you can use DP here (knapsack one) but the issue is that it will TLE and MLE both,It is O(n^2) and since constraint are 2*10^5, 2 state dp would also fail, u can optimize this to 1 state DP array so MLE can be cleared but TLE cant be helped i guess.

      lambdadelta
      4 weeks ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      lambdadelta
      4 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Let dp[i]=max answer if we select pairs such that the min y is equal to i.

      Then answer for dp[i] can be calculated by using a priority queue (I'm not stating my implementation here, but basically for dp[i] I'm finding the max sum of x values for pairs of type (x,y) with y>=i)

      Then the problem says we have to find the answer for each pair in 'b' if we had that pair. So I consider 2 cases:

      1) we have the pair, but we don't use it: Then answer is simply max(dp[i]) as nothing changes.

      2) We may use it: Let's say the pair is (X,Y). If I include this pair in my array a, then dp[i] for i>Y won't change. But dp[i] for i<=Y may change. Because,

      (i) it may be possible that the min values out of the selected i values (for some dp[i]) may be less than X

      (ii) it may be possible that dp[i] was calculated with us having selected less than i values, which means there's room for more elements to add.

      So I just maintain some values in order to find the max for each (X,Y).

      But it gives WA on TC2, so I'm wrong somewhere. But idk where, it seems alright to me.

      → Reply

Thank you for the editorial! Isn't code 1 for C incorrect?

What is wrong

We erase from s, but compare j to n (the initial length), so we will inevitably get out of bounds -> erase will crash if we were to call it. We should compare to s.size() instead.

Undetermined21
4 weeks ago, show (+2) #
»
Undetermined21
4 weeks ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+4

O(n) solution for C

As explained in the editorial, it is always optimal to delete a pair when you find one. You can avoid looping through the string multiple times by mantaining a stack with all currently undeleted characters. Loop through characters in the string from start to end. When you encounter character cc in the string, if it matches the top element of the stack, tt, then cc and tt will eventually become adjacent after a bunch of deletions, so remove tt from the stack. Otherwise, add cc to the stack. If the stack is empty after looping through the string, it's possible to turn the string into stars.

int n;
string s;
cin >> n >> s;
stack<char> st;
for (char c : s) {
	if (!st.empty() && st.top() == c)
		st.pop();
	else
		st.push(c);
}
cout << (st.empty() ? "YES\n" : "NO\n");

For G bonus: I suppose O(nlog2(3))O(nlog2⁡(3)) can pass with some minor optimizations.

I have an idea for an o(nlogn)o(nlog⁡n) solution for F, which is just improvising O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n) parts with o(nlogn)o(nlog⁡n) parts, e.g. replacing standard sorting with radix sorting, and using e.g. a vEB tree to manage the set. Even though I'm not sure it'll run better since O(nlog(n))O(nlog⁡(n)) with a simple heap is already 100ms.

F was pretty good, not some boring DP like the last few rounds, wasn't brutal but hard enough for me to think.

0

A little bit different solution for Task E: -

I used a simulation-based thought process, focusing on the explicit sequences each player tries to construct: -

We know that: -

  • Alice wants to make the array unsorted. Her goal is to create a state where Ai>AjAi>Aj for some i<ji<j.

  • Bob wants to keep the array sorted. His goal is the exact opposite, ensuring Ai≤Ai+1Ai≤Ai+1 for all valid ii.

  • Because both players play optimally, for a composite number CC with prime factors as p1≤p2≤⋯≤pkp1≤p2≤⋯≤pk is changed in a specific arrangement, by alternately distributing the prime factors into a left and right partition (and reversing the right), the split naturally SS is increasing first, then decreasing. Mathematically, this is like a bitonic sequence that looks like this: —

S=(p2,p4,…,ppeak,…,p3,p1)S=(p2,p4,…,ppeak,…,p3,p1)

Combining these observations, we can construct the final modified sequence by replacing every composite number with their respective expanded sequences.

Once we create this fully expanded sequence, our final check is very simple: If the final modified sequence is non-decreasing, Bob wins. Otherwise, Alice wins. There's also a base condition that if the initial sequence is non-decreasing in the first place, then Bob wins.

I hope this helps! :)

Submission Link

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    srinadh777
    4 weeks ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    srinadh777
    4 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    But for every element alice can't start first then how is this possible for every element. S=(p2,p4,…,ppeak,…,p3,p1)

    for instance: 16 10 25 if alice choose 16 first, next bob choose 10. So, Definitely alice need to choose 10 and split like 5*2, then only alice can win.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      mox
      4 weeks ago, show #
      »
      »
      »
      mox
      4 weeks ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      you’re correct about the part that alice must choose to split 10, and that’s exactly why she would never start with 16 as it’s the sub-optimal choice, simply because 16’s a perfect power of 2, similar to how 25 is a perfect power of 5!

      simply put, the simulation can be seen as finding a particular composite number, whose factorisation can be made non-decreasing, since one such number is enough to guarantee alice’s victory, the rest of the simulation isn’t actually necessary.

      moreover, it doesn’t make our answer incorrect at any point simply because of the fact that if there exists a winning move for alice, she can do that move first and then play the entire game with bob just for the sake of it (simulation), in case there is no winning move for her then again the simulation can’t generate a winning position and hence bob wins!

      → Reply

I was doing the contest and I had a question about problem C. In the solution logic, we delete characters in pairs, but if we have a case like "llml", what happens? In theory, the answer should be YES, but when running the algorithm it gives NO, someone can explain to me?

    The answer is no because when there is no more movements all characters should have be changed to * (eliminated), in your example, the only two scenarios are the following:

    1. llml -> **ml -> game ends, not all characters are * -> AksLolCoding lost
    2. llml -> *lm* -> game ends, not all characters are * -> AksLolCoding lost

for the problem C: lets assume that our string is special and indexes are 1,2,3,...,n. Then we can pair these indexes such that for any pair (i,j) that they selected in some operation. (which means s[i]=s[j]). Then for any pair (a,b) and (c,d) its impossible to be like a<c<b<d. That explains why the order of the operation does not matter since our string should be concatenation of some even length palindrome strings.

0
Even Palindrome like solution for C

...A lot of cool solutions for C, I just thought of it as an Even Palindrome problem. Used 2 adjacent pointers(i,j) and if they were equal then converted both to '*' & started deacreasing i and increasing j, if string[i/j] is a '*' then just ignore it and decrease/increase i/j respectively till the j > string.size().

int t = 0;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        int n = 0;
        cin >> n;
        string s = "";
        cin >> s;
        // code for even palindrome llmllm
        int i = 0;
        int j = 1;
        while(i >= 0 && j < s.size()){
            while(i >= 0 && j < s.size() && s[i] == s[j]){
                s[i] = '*';
                s[j] = '*';
                i--;
                j++;
                while(i >= 0 && s[i] == '*')
                    i--;
                while(j < s.size() && s[j] == '*')
                    j++;
            }
            if(j > s.size() - 1)
                break;
            i = j;
            j++;
        }
        bool ans = true;
        for(int i = 0; i < s.size(); i++){
            if(s[i] != '*')
                ans = false;
        }
        debug(s);
        ans ? cout << "YES" : cout << "NO";
        cout << endl;
→ Reply
